_metadata.source: UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage (ICH) Representative List
_metadata.description: All UNESCO ICH inscribed elements related to carnivals, masquerades, and related festive traditions. Includes European and global entries.
notes: Entries with status 'removed' were previously on the Representative List but removed by subsequent Committee decisions.
Patum of Berga (Spain) is included as a festive procession with carnival-like masked characters.
Processional giants (Belgium/France) overlap with carnival traditions in many towns.

entries.name: Carnival of Oruro
entries.description: Ten-day carnival at 3,700m altitude in western Bolivia. Main event is the entrada procession with over 28,000 dancers and 10,000 musicians in ~50 groups. The traditional llama llama (diablada) dance blends Andean religion with Christian liturgy. Gives rise to a panoply of popular arts in masks, textiles and embroidery.

entries.name: Carnival of Binche
entries.description: One of Europe's oldest surviving street carnivals, with roots in the Middle Ages. The Gille characters — wearing ostrich-feather hats, wooden clogs, bells and wax masks — parade through the town on Mardi Gras. Pierrots, harlequins and peasants follow, culminating with the Gilles' dancing in the Grand Place under fireworks.
entries.timing: Three days preceding Lent (Shrove Sunday through Mardi Gras)

entries.name: Carnival of Barranquilla
entries.description: Four-day carnival on Colombia's Caribbean coast blending European, African, and indigenous cultural traditions. Features cumbia music, animal masks, floats, costumes and theatrical performances. Groups of masqueraded dancers, actors, singers and instrumentalists satirize contemporary political life.

entries.name: Processional giants and dragons in Belgium and France
entries.description: Traditional processions of huge effigies of giants, animals or dragons dating from the 14th century. Giants measure up to 9 metres and weigh up to 350 kilos. They represent mythical heroes, historical/legendary characters and trades. Each effigy has its specific feast day. Note: Ducasse of Ath removed from this element in 2022.
entries.timing: Various dates throughout the year (each effigy has a specific feast day)
entries.modification_note: Ducasse of Ath removed from element in December 2022 (Decision 17.COM 8.a)

entries.name: Patum of Berga
entries.description: Popular festival in this Catalan town north of Barcelona with origins in medieval Corpus Christi festivities. Characters include fire demons (Plens), mule dragons (Guites), papier mâché horses (Cavallets), an eagle, giant-headed dwarves, and giants dressed as Saracens. All perform the final dance, the Tirabol, with fireworks and music.
entries.timing: Week of Corpus Christi (late May to late June)

entries.name: Ijele masquerade
entries.description: Performed in Anambra state communities at celebrations, burial ceremonies and special occasions in the dry season. The mask is ~4 metres tall, constructed of colourful fabric on bamboo sticks with figurines, divided by a large python. The mask carrier is chosen by ballot and secludes for 3 months beforehand. The masquerade serves spiritual, political and cultural roles.
entries.timing: Dry season celebrations, burial ceremonies and special occasions

entries.name: Annual carnival bell ringers' pageant from the Kastav area
entries.description: During the January carnival period, bell ringers march through villages in the Kastav region wearing sheepskin throws with bells, sporting hats with evergreen sprigs. Groups of 2–30 ringers swagger behind a guide, bumping each other's hips to ring the bells in unison. At each village they form concentric circles, ringing fiercely until residents offer food and rest. At carnival's end they burn collected rubbish in front of each house.
entries.timing: January carnival period (17 January to Ash Wednesday, with Sunday pageants)

entries.name: Busó festivities at Mohács: masked end-of-winter carnival custom
entries.description: Six-day carnival in late February marking the end of winter. Over 500 busós — frightening-looking costumed figures in wooden masks and woolly cloaks — arrive by rowboat on the Danube and march through the city. A coffin symbolizing winter is burned on a bonfire in the central square. Originated with the Croatian minority in Mohács.

entries.name: Carnaval de Negros y Blancos
entries.description: From native Andean and Hispanic traditions, this celebration in Pasto lasts from 28 December to 6 January. Begins with the Carnival of Water; includes the Old Year's parade with satirical burning figures. On the main days, people don black cosmetics then white talcum to symbolize equality and integrate all citizens through a celebration of ethnic and cultural difference.

entries.name: Shrovetide door-to-door processions and masks in the villages of the Hlinecko area
entries.description: Village men and boys wearing masks (red for boys, black for married men) go door-to-door with a brass band. Four men perform a ritual dance at each house to ensure a rich harvest. A symbolic 'Killing of the Mare' ritual takes place at the last house. The processions were banned by the Catholic Church in the 18th–19th centuries and by the socialist government in the 20th century.

entries.name: Aalst carnival
entries.description: Three-day carnival beginning Sunday before Lent in East Flanders. Features a Prince Carnaval who symbolically becomes mayor; a procession of effigies; a broom dance; a parade of men dressed as women; and a ritual effigy burning. The 600-year-old ritual draws up to 100,000 spectators. Removed from the UNESCO list in 2019 following antisemitic displays at the carnival.
entries.timing: Three days beginning Sunday before Lent
entries.removal_reason: Removed in 2019 (Decision 14.COM 12) following antisemitic and racist representations at the event

entries.name: Carnival of Granville
entries.description: Four-day celebration preceding Shrove Tuesday in coastal Normandy. Begins with the mayor handing keys to King Carnival, then float processions with marching bands. About 40 floats made by 2,500 'carnivalists' over six months humorously satirize current events and celebrities. Ends with a 'night of intrigues' and the king's cremation in the port. Attracts 100,000 spectators annually.

entries.name: Carnival of El Callao, a festive representation of a memory and cultural identity
entries.description: Associated with cannes brulées emancipation celebrations from French-speaking Caribbean islands. January–March, featuring parades of up to 3,000 people in historical and fantasy costumes. Key characters: madamas (Antillean matrons in colourful dresses); medio-pintos (young people smudging charcoal on non-donors); mineros (gold miners); and diablos (masked people with whips maintaining order).

entries.name: Basel Carnival
entries.description: Largest carnival in Switzerland, lasting exactly 72 hours from the Monday after Ash Wednesday. Two parades bring together 11,000 costumed carnivalists in fife and drum cliques, floats and carriages. Tuesday is children's day. About 20,000 active participants; ~200,000 visitors. The carnival contributes to social cohesion and promotes tolerance through social criticism. Transmitted through 'clique' associations.
entries.timing: 72 hours from Monday after Ash Wednesday

entries.name: Winter festivities, Carnival of Podence
entries.description: Social practice originally a rite of passage for men, now extended to women and children. The Caretos — wearing tinplate or leather masks and costumes with colourful wool fringes and small bells — dance around women with their cowbells during the three-day street festival. Monday night features theatrical satire; Shrove Tuesday ends with the burning of a Shrovetide figure.
entries.timing: Three days: Shrove Sunday to Shrove Tuesday

entries.name: Bear festivities in the Pyrenees
entries.description: Winter festivities in five Pyrenean villages in France and Andorra. Young men dress as bears and run through the streets trying to catch participants. The scenario symbolizes the end of winter, spring rebirth, and the human-nature relationship. Festivities are punctuated by dances, speeches in Catalan, and music. After declining in the 1960s, the festivities have regained popularity over the last 30 years.

entries.name: Shakespeare Mas', a traditional component of Carriacou's annual carnival
entries.description: Shakespeare Mas' combines speechmaking, costume design and public theatre. Practitioners called 'kings' compete by reciting passages from Shakespeare's Julius Caesar, historical texts and Bible verses. Incorrect recitations are met with a symbolic lash on a padded headpiece. A 100-year-old practice rooted in the diverse cultures brought together under slavery. Now includes women and girls, transmitted through school curricula.

entries.name: Carnival of Binche — note: RL/00690 redirects to main ICH page
entries.note: RL/00690 appears to be an outdated or redirect URL. The canonical Carnival of Binche entry is at RL/carnival-of-binche-00033

related_entries.note: Seto Leelo (Estonia) is not a carnival but was confirmed as a separate traditional singing element when checking RL/00173

related_entries.name: Community safeguarding and documenting of the 'Lastovo Poklad' carnival custom
related_entries.description: Registered as a Good Safeguarding Practice (Art. 18) in 2025. The Lastovo Poklad is a carnival custom on the island of Lastovo in Croatia.

search_api_notes.note: The UNESCO ICH website uses bot protection (CAPTCHA/F5 BIG-IP) that blocks direct curl requests. The pplx_sdk content.fetch tool was used to retrieve page content. The AJAX search endpoint also returned CAPTCHA pages when accessed directly.
search_api_notes.working_approach: Use pplx_sdk.content.fetch with known RL/ URLs, or search via pplx_sdk.search.web with domains=['ich.unesco.org']
